Question: The single phase equivalent circuit of a three-phase, four pole induction motor is shown in
Figure 1. For the purposes of this problem we will assume armature resistance is negligible.
The machine is connected to a three-phase voltage source with line-neutral voltage of 200
volts, RMS (346 volts, line-line) and a frequency of 400 radians/second. The motor reaches
peak torque at a speed of 160 Radians/second (about 1528 RPM).
What is the value of that peak torque (in N-m)?
after_value: The single phase equivalent circuit of a three-phase, four pole induction motor is shown in Figure 1. For the purposes of this problem we will assume armature resistance is negligible. The machine is connected to a three-phase voltage source with line-neutral voltage of 200 volts RMS (346 volts line-line) and a frequency of 400 radians/second. The motor reaches peak torque at a speed of 160 radians/second (about 1528 RPM). What is the value of that peak torque (in N-m)?

before_value: A lossless microstrip transmission line consists of a trace of width \( w \). It is drawn over a practically infinite ground plane and is separated by a dielectric slab of thickness \( t \) and relative permittivity \( \varepsilon_r > 1 \). The inductance per unit length and the characteristic impedance of this line are \( L \) and \( Z_0 \), respectively. Which one of the following inequalities is always satisfied?
after_value: A lossless microstrip transmission line consists of a trace of width \( w \). It is drawn over a practically infinite ground plane and is separated by a dielectric slab of thickness \( t \) and relative permittivity \( \varepsilon_r > 1 \). The inductance per unit length and the characteristic impedance of this line are \( L \) and \( Z_0 \), respectively. Which one of the following inequalities is always satisfied?

before_value: \( Z_0 < \sqrt{\frac{Lt}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r w}} \)

Question: The figure in <image> illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks. Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).
The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \). Both tanks have heights of 1 m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty. Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s
after_value:

Both tanks have heights of 1 m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s
